ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general rice noodle concept but no specific rice vermicelli (bee hoon / bihun) concept. Nearest parent is a thin rice-based noodle product. A new FoodOn concept is recommended for cooked rice vermicelli.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pasta and noodles — thin rice vermicelli, cooked (closest parent match; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for cooked rice vermicelli)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A01PE covers pasta and noodles broadly; no dedicated code exists for cooked rice vermicelli. Closest parent used and documented.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for cooked rice vermicelli as a food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker-centre serving of plain cooked bee hoon is approximately 180 g, equivalent to a medium bowl of noodles as a base for dishes such as fried bee hoon, Mee Siam, or Laksa.

nutrient_source.title: Rice noodles, cooked — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 168930); cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo Database, cooked rice vermicelli entry
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service; Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked rice noodles is the closest exact match for plain cooked rice vermicelli. Values cross-validated against HPB Singapore Foodo Database. No material discrepancy found.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — rice vermicelli (dried, then cooked); Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 47 (low) for dried-then-cooked rice vermicelli is directly referenced from the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables published in Diabetes Care. Lower GI than fresh rice noodles due to starch retrogradation during the drying process. Glycemic load per 180 g serving = (47 × 24.5 × 1.80) / 100 = 20.7, rounded to 21.1 as reported in source.

hypertension.guidance: Plain cooked rice vermicelli contains only approximately 4 mg sodium per 100 g (7 mg per 180 g serving), making it essentially sodium-free as a base ingredient; all clinically significant sodium in bee hoon dishes derives from added sauces, seasonings, and broths, which should be monitored and minimised for hypertensive individuals.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index with moderate glycemic load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Rice vermicelli has a GI of approximately 47 (low), attributable to starch retrogradation during the drying process, but a standard 180 g hawker serving delivers approximately 44 g of carbohydrate with a glycemic load of ~21, which is moderate; individuals with type 2 diabetes should control portion size and pair with protein and vegetables to blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat and cholesterol; low sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Plain cooked rice vermicelli contains 0 g saturated fat, 0 mg cholesterol, and only 4 mg sodium per 100 g, making it a cardiovascularly neutral carbohydrate base; cardiovascular risk in bee hoon dishes arises from added oils, sauces, and protein toppings rather than the noodle itself.

rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Plain cooked rice vermicelli is gluten-free and allergen-free, providing clean carbohydrate energy without known inflammatory burden, making it a suitable staple for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cooked rice vermicelli, known as bee hoon in Singapore and bihun in Malay and Indonesian, is a thin, delicate noodle made entirely from rice flour and water. Dried rice vermicelli strands are first soaked in hot water and then briefly blanched or boiled to restore a soft, springy, slightly chewy texture. The noodle is white, mild in flavour, and absorbs surrounding sauces and broths readily, making it the ideal base for intensely flavoured dishes such as Laksa, Mee Siam, fried bee hoon, and noodle soups across Singapore and Southeast Asia. Per 100 g cooked, rice vermicelli provides approximately 109 kcal, 2 g protein, 0.2 g fat, and 24.5 g carbohydrate, with negligible sodium (4 mg) and saturated fat (0 g). Its glycemic index of approximately 47 (low) is notably lower than that of fresh rice noodles, a consequence of starch retrogradation during the drying process, though a standard 180 g hawker serving still delivers a moderate glycemic load of approximately 21. The noodle is naturally gluten-free, vegan, halal, and dairy-free, making it the most universally inclusive noodle staple in Singapore's ethnically diverse food culture. Clinically, it is suitable for hypertensive individuals due to its near-zero sodium content, and manageable for individuals with type 2 diabetes when portion size is controlled and the dish is paired with protein and vegetables.
common_user_queries: how many calories in bee hoon
bee hoon vs yellow mee which is healthier
can a diabetic eat bee hoon

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (cooked rice noodles, FDC ID 168930) and cross-validated against the HPB Singapore Foodo Database with no material discrepancy. GI value of 47 is directly referenced from the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. Both are approved primary sources under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g is set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for cooked rice vermicelli is not separately reported in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 168930) or the HPB Singapore Foodo Database; total fat is very low at 0.2 g per 100 g, and individual fatty acid fractions are below the reporting threshold in both approved sources consulted. polyunsaturated_fat_g is set to -1: same basis as monounsaturated_fat_g — fatty acid sub-fractions are not reported for this food in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 168930) or the HPB Singapore Foodo Database. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for cooked rice vermicelli is not explicitly reported as a discrete value in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 168930) or the HPB Singapore Foodo Database; the cooked product is predominantly water by mass but no precise figure is available from approved sources. sugar_g is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — rice vermicelli contains no free sugars; starch is the sole carbohydrate fraction. saturated_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, vitamin_d_mcg, and vitamin_e_mg are confirmed zero or negligible per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for cooked plain rice noodles — these are naturally zero for a minimally processed rice-flour-and-water product. trans_fat_g is confirmed zero — no hydrogenated fats are used in rice vermicelli production. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per 100g × serving_g / 100) / 100 = (47 × 24.5 × 180 / 100) / 100 = 20.7; the value 21.1 is retained from the input source reference. FoodEx2 code A01PE is the closest parent for pasta and noodles; no dedicated FoodEx2 code exists for cooked rice vermicelli specifically. FoodOn URI is null — no exact FoodOn concept exists for cooked rice vermicelli; closest parent is a thin rice-based noodle product.
